costs: Recurring funding cost for outreach/preparation programs targeted at disadvantaged feeder schools
Potential resistance from advantaged-family constituencies perceiving quotas as reducing merit-based access
Church/private maintainers may decline voluntary quota adoption given their exemption from state admissions rules
content.equity_impact: Likely positive but narrow: improves compositional access for disadvantaged students to existing high-value-added tracks (moderating the 14%-vs-1% top-performer gap) but does not address system-wide segregation drivers or the contested long-run individual benefit of track attendance.
expected_benefits: Directly increases disadvantaged representation in high-value-added tracks without triggering the structural-abolition political backlash
Uses existing assessment infrastructure for monitoring, minimizing new administrative build cost
Complements rather than substitutes for broader desegregation efforts (T4) or admissions redesign (T3)
content.goal: Preserve the 6-/8-year track structure and admissions exam but introduce mandated disadvantaged-student quotas, outreach, and exam-preparation support to change who is selected without changing the selection mechanism's timing or structural design.

implementation_steps.action: Mandate disadvantaged-student admission quotas or bonus-point systems for state-maintained gimnáziums, and negotiate voluntary adoption with church/private maintainers
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education and Klebelsberg Centre

implementation_steps.action: Fund targeted exam-preparation and outreach programs in disadvantaged feeder primary schools ahead of grade-5/7 selection points
Monitor compositional outcomes annually using existing National ABC/PISA infrastructure disaggregated by SES and Roma status

mechanisms: Since per-pupil financing is currently identical across elite and disadvantaged schools yet access depends on private tutoring, targeted supplementary funding for exam preparation for disadvantaged applicants could offset the tutoring-access gap without altering exam content

Quota mechanisms directly address the compositional imbalance (14% advantaged vs 1% deprived among top readers) by mandating minimum disadvantaged representation, independent of the admissions-design reform in T3

School starting age evidence shows kindergarten-stage interventions produce stronger equity gains for low-status children than later interventions, suggesting outreach should begin well before the age-10 selection point to be effective
risks: Value-added gains attributed to selective tracks may be substantially explained by family background rather than institutional effect, meaning quota-admitted students might not experience the same benefit unless underlying school-quality issues are also addressed

Zero measured tertiary-education effect of track selection in at least one study raises the possibility that expanding access to a track with contested added value may not durably benefit admitted disadvantaged students
Quotas without broader distrust-of-local-schools resolution may not shift underlying parental sorting behavior driving demand for selective tracks
content.title: Targeted quota and outreach expansion for disadvantaged access to existing tracks
